_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Pericardial effusion developing in any clinical condition primarily involving pericardium (commonly viral or bacterial)
Pericardial effusion secondary to systemic disorders such as malignancies, pulmonary tuberculosis, chronic renal failure, thyroid diseases, and autoimmune diseases
Cardiac tamponade, a medical emergency where fluid is accumulated under pressure in the pericardial cavity, resulting in compression of cardiac chambers and impeding cardiac filling
Symptoms including dyspnoea on exertion progressing to orthopnoea, chest pain and/or fullness
Local compression symptoms such as nausea (diaphragm), dysphagia (oesophagus), hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve) and hiccups (phrenic nerve)
Non-specific symptoms including cough, weakness, fatigue, anorexia and palpitations
Signs of tamponade including elevated jugular venous pressure, pulsus paradoxus, hypotension and diminished heart sounds
Transthoracic echocardiography is the most important tool for diagnosis, grading, guidance of pericardial fluid aspiration, and follow up of a patient with pericardial effusion.

preauth.label: Echo/ color Doppler report with stills

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure
Was the Echo/ color doppler report suggestive of Pericardial effusion/ Cardiac tamponade?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed Procedure / Operative notes submitted?
Did the post procedure echo/ colour doppler report confirm decrease in volume of pericardial fluid?
Is there a detailed discharge Summary mentioning date of follow-up submitted?
Is the analysis report of removed pericardial fluid submitted?

common_queries: Echo/color Doppler report not suggestive of pericardial effusion or cardiac tamponade
Clinical notes are missing or do not detail history, signs & symptoms, or indication for the procedure
Procedure/Operative notes are missing or not detailed
Post-procedure echo/colour Doppler report does not confirm a decrease in the volume of pericardial fluid
Detailed Discharge Summary is missing or does not mention the date of follow-up
Analysis report of removed pericardial fluid is missing

additional_information.special_empanelment: Functional Cardiac Cath lab/ General Surgery OT
clinical_key_pointers: Pericardial effusion etiology: commonly viral or bacterial, or secondary to malignancies, pulmonary tuberculosis, chronic renal failure, thyroid diseases, and autoimmune diseases.
In developed world, idiopathic is commonest etiology followed by tumour, while in developing countries like India, tuberculosis remains a leading cause besides idiopathic.
Cardiac tamponade is a medical emergency where fluid accumulated under pressure in pericardial cavity, compressing cardiac chambers and impeding filling.

Tamponade can develop without large pericardial effusion. Clinicians should correlate echocardiographic findings (right ventricular/atrial collapse, respiratory variation of mitral/tricuspid flow) with clinical signs (hypotension, pulsus paradoxus).
Transthoracic echocardiography is the most important tool for diagnosis, grading, guidance of pericardial fluid aspiration (pericardiocentesis), and follow-up.
Rapid pericardiocentesis is a life-saving procedure in cardiac tamponade.
A significant proportion of patients with pericardial effusion are asymptomatic, often an incidental finding on X-ray or echocardiogram.
Classic symptoms include dyspnoea on exertion progressing to orthopnoea, chest pain and/or fullness.
Additional occasional symptoms due to local compression may include nausea (diaphragm), dysphagia (oesophagus), hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve) and hiccups (phrenic nerve).
Non-specific symptoms include cough, weakness, fatigue, anorexia and palpitations.
Physical examination may be normal in patients without haemodynamic compromise.
When tamponade develops, classic signs include elevated jugular venous pressure, pulsus paradoxus, hypotension and diminished heart sounds.
